title: Infrastructure Positioning
content:
Joz approaches infrastructure as the production foundation that makes AI and agentic systems scalable, secure, observable, resilient, and repeatable.

Infrastructure is not the intelligence layer. It is the operating environment that keeps APIs, agents, workflows, data stores, execution services, and verification systems available and trustworthy.

title: Core Infrastructure Architecture
content:
User → DNS/CDN → Load Balancer or API Gateway → FastAPI/API Services → Agent Workers → Tools and Domain Services → Databases and External Systems

Supporting layers:
Containers
Kubernetes
Workflow durability
Caching
Event transport
Secrets management
Observability
CI/CD
Security controls
Disaster recovery

title: Cloud
content:
Cloud provides compute, networking, storage, managed databases, queues, identity, observability, and deployment services.

Typical providers:
AWS
Microsoft Azure
Google Cloud Platform

Cloud = where the platform runs.
It should be designed for availability, isolation, cost control, scalability, and recoverability.

title: Docker
content:
Docker packages an application and its dependencies into a portable container image.

Docker solves environment consistency:
The same image can run locally, in testing, and in production.

Docker = shipping container for software.
It packages the service, runtime, libraries, and configuration required to run.

title: Kubernetes
content:
Kubernetes deploys, schedules, scales, restarts, and manages containers across multiple machines.

Core responsibilities:
Pod scheduling
Horizontal scaling
Health checks
Self-healing
Service discovery
Rolling deployments
Configuration and secret injection
Resource allocation
Traffic routing

Docker packages services.
Kubernetes runs and manages them.

title: Kubernetes Pods
content:
A Kubernetes Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes.

A pod usually contains one application container, such as:
FastAPI service
Agent worker
Background job
Tool service

Pods are disposable. Kubernetes replaces unhealthy pods automatically.

title: Kubernetes Deployment
content:
A Kubernetes Deployment defines how many copies of a service should run and how updates should be rolled out.

Example:
FastAPI Deployment = 6 replicas
Agent Worker Deployment = 20 replicas

Deployments support rolling updates, rollback, scaling, and self-healing.

title: Kubernetes Service
content:
A Kubernetes Service gives a stable network address to a changing group of pods.

Pods can be replaced or rescheduled, but the service address remains stable.

Service = internal network endpoint for a group of pods.

title: Ingress and API Gateway
content:
Ingress routes external HTTP traffic into Kubernetes services.

An API Gateway adds:
Authentication
Rate limiting
Request routing
Versioning
Quotas
Traffic policies
Observability

Ingress = traffic entry into the cluster.
API Gateway = managed front door and control layer for APIs.

title: Load Balancer
content:
A load balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple service instances.

Benefits:
Higher availability
Horizontal scaling
Failure isolation
Better resource use

Load Balancer = receptionist directing users to available servers.

title: Horizontal and Vertical Scaling
content:
Horizontal scaling = add more service instances.
Vertical scaling = add more CPU or memory to one machine.

Modern distributed systems primarily use horizontal scaling for stateless APIs and workers.

Example:
1 FastAPI instance → 10 FastAPI instances
5 Agent workers → 50 Agent workers

title: Autoscaling
content:
Autoscaling adjusts compute capacity based on demand.

Signals can include:
CPU
Memory
Request rate
Queue depth
Workflow backlog
Latency
Custom business metrics

Use autoscaling carefully for agent systems because LLM latency, token usage, and external API limits can create nonlinear load.

title: Stateless Services
content:
A stateless service does not depend on local machine memory between requests.

State is stored externally in:
PostgreSQL
Redis
Object storage
Workflow engines

Stateless services are easier to scale, restart, and move across machines.

title: PostgreSQL
content:
PostgreSQL is the primary relational database for durable application state.

Typical data:
Users
Permissions
Workflow state
Agent runs
Orders
Audit records
Configuration
Metadata

PostgreSQL = permanent filing system.

title: PostgreSQL High Availability
content:
Production PostgreSQL should use:
Replication
Automated backups
Point-in-time recovery
Failover
Connection pooling
Read replicas where needed
Schema migrations
Monitoring

The database is often the most important stateful dependency in the platform.

title: Redis
content:
Redis is an in-memory data store used for low-latency access.

Typical uses:
Caching
Sessions
Rate limits
Distributed locks
Short-lived workflow state
Queues
Pub/sub

Redis = desk drawer.
PostgreSQL = warehouse.

title: Caching
content:
Caching stores frequently used data closer to the application.

Benefits:
Lower latency
Reduced database load
Reduced external API cost
Higher throughput

Risks:
Stale data
Incorrect invalidation
Memory pressure
Inconsistent state

Never treat cache as the source of truth.

title: Kafka and NATS
content:
Kafka and NATS move events between services asynchronously.

Kafka is suited to durable event streams, replay, high throughput, and analytics pipelines.
NATS is suited to lightweight, low-latency messaging and request-reply patterns.

Event transport decouples services and allows independent scaling.

title: Event-Driven Architecture
content:
Event-driven architecture lets services publish events instead of calling every downstream service directly.

Example:
TradeExecuted event
→ Portfolio updater
→ Risk updater
→ Audit service
→ Notification service
→ Analytics pipeline

This improves decoupling, scalability, and recoverability.

title: Queues and Workers
content:
Queues separate request intake from background processing.

Flow:
API receives request
→ Queue
→ Worker processes task
→ Result stored
→ User notified

Queues are useful for:
Long-running agent tasks
Document ingestion
Embedding generation
Model evaluation
Batch processing
Notifications

title: Temporal
content:
Temporal provides durable workflow execution.

It handles:
Retries
Timeouts
Approval waits
Long-running workflows
Crash recovery
State reconstruction
Compensation logic

LangGraph orchestrates reasoning.
Temporal keeps execution reliable.

title: Secrets Management
content:
Secrets must not be stored in source code, Docker images, logs, or plain environment files.

Use:
HashiCorp Vault
AWS KMS
AWS Secrets Manager
Azure Key Vault
Google Secret Manager

Secrets include:
API keys
Database credentials
Signing keys
Certificates
Tokens

title: Workload Identity
content:
Workload identity lets services prove who they are without sharing long-lived static credentials.

Examples:
AWS IAM roles for workloads
Kubernetes service accounts
OIDC-based federation
Short-lived tokens

Workload identity is safer than embedding shared secrets in services.

title: Network Security
content:
Network security should use:
Private subnets
Firewalls and security groups
TLS everywhere
Service-to-service authentication
Network policies
Restricted egress
DDoS protection
Zero-trust assumptions

Only necessary services should be publicly reachable.

title: Service Mesh
content:
A service mesh manages service-to-service communication.

Capabilities can include:
Mutual TLS
Traffic policies
Retries
Circuit breaking
Observability
Service identity

Examples:
Istio
Linkerd

Use a service mesh only when complexity justifies it.

title: Observability
content:
Observability combines logs, metrics, and traces.

Logs = what happened.
Metrics = how the system behaves over time.
Traces = how one request moves across services.

For AI systems, also trace:
Prompts
Model calls
Tool calls
Token usage
Latency
Cost
Policy decisions
Errors
Final outcomes

title: OpenTelemetry
content:
OpenTelemetry is an open standard for collecting traces, metrics, and logs.

It can follow a request across:
Frontend
API Gateway
FastAPI
LangGraph
Tools
Databases
External APIs
Execution services

OpenTelemetry = system-wide instrumentation layer.

title: Prometheus and Grafana
content:
Prometheus collects time-series metrics.
Grafana visualizes dashboards and alerts.

Typical metrics:
Request rate
Error rate
Latency
CPU and memory
Queue depth
Database health
Agent success rate
Tool failure rate
Token cost

title: LangSmith
content:
LangSmith provides tracing, evaluation, and debugging for LLM and agent workflows.

It can record:
Prompts
Model responses
Agent transitions
Tool calls
Latency
Token usage
Failures
Evaluation scores

LangSmith complements, but does not replace, infrastructure observability.

title: CI/CD
content:
CI/CD automates software integration, testing, security checks, packaging, and deployment.

Typical flow:
Commit → Build → Unit Tests → Integration Tests → Security Scan → Docker Image → Deploy → Verify

CI = Continuous Integration.
CD = Continuous Delivery or Deployment.

title: CI/CD Tools
content:
Common CI/CD tools:
GitHub Actions
GitLab CI
Jenkins
CircleCI
Azure DevOps
Argo CD

A production pipeline should support approvals, rollback, environment promotion, and auditability.

title: Infrastructure as Code
content:
Infrastructure as Code defines cloud resources through version-controlled code.

Tools:
Terraform
Pulumi
AWS CloudFormation

Benefits:
Repeatability
Reviewability
Auditability
Environment consistency
Automated provisioning
Disaster recovery

title: GitOps
content:
GitOps uses Git as the source of truth for infrastructure and deployment state.

Flow:
Change configuration in Git
→ Review
→ Merge
→ Automated reconciliation
→ Cluster updated

Argo CD and Flux are common GitOps tools.

title: Blue-Green and Canary Deployment
content:
Blue-green deployment switches traffic between old and new environments.
Canary deployment sends a small percentage of traffic to the new version first.

Both reduce deployment risk and support safer rollback.

title: Health Checks
content:
Health checks tell the platform whether a service is alive and ready.

Liveness check = should the process be restarted?
Readiness check = can the service receive traffic?

Kubernetes uses these checks for self-healing and safe routing.

title: Retries and Backoff
content:
Retries should use bounded attempts, exponential backoff, and jitter.

Uncontrolled retries can overload failing systems and create cascading failures.

Retry only operations that are safe and idempotent.

title: Circuit Breaker
content:
A circuit breaker stops repeated calls to an unhealthy dependency.

States:
Closed = calls allowed
Open = calls blocked
Half-open = limited test calls

Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures.

title: Idempotency
content:
Idempotency ensures repeated execution of the same request produces one logical result.

It is essential for:
Payments
Orders
Transactions
Workflow retries
Webhook processing

Use idempotency keys and durable records.

title: Backpressure
content:
Backpressure prevents the system from accepting more work than it can safely process.

Techniques:
Queue limits
Rate limits
Concurrency caps
Load shedding
Admission control
Priority queues

Backpressure protects latency and system stability.

title: Rate Limiting
content:
Rate limiting controls how many requests a user, service, or agent can make.

It protects:
APIs
Model providers
Databases
Tool services
Trading systems

Limits can be based on requests, tokens, cost, or risk.

title: Disaster Recovery
content:
Disaster recovery prepares the platform for major failures.

Core practices:
Backups
Cross-region replication
Recovery procedures
Failover testing
Infrastructure as Code
Defined RTO and RPO

RTO = maximum acceptable recovery time.
RPO = maximum acceptable data loss.

title: High Availability
content:
High availability reduces downtime through redundancy and failure isolation.

Use:
Multiple service replicas
Multi-zone deployment
Database failover
Load balancing
Health checks
Automated recovery
Graceful degradation

title: Cost Management
content:
Infrastructure must be cost-aware.

Control costs through:
Autoscaling
Right-sizing
Caching
Model routing
Spot instances where safe
Storage lifecycle policies
Request quotas
Cost observability
Environment shutdown schedules

Cost is an architecture constraint, not an afterthought.

title: Environment Strategy
content:
Use separate environments:
Local
Development
Testing
Staging
Production

Production access should be restricted.
Configuration should be environment-specific.
Promotions should follow controlled CI/CD workflows.

title: Agent Infrastructure Scaling
content:
Scale agent systems by separating:
API intake
Reasoning workers
Tool services
Embedding workers
Evaluation workers
Execution services
Verification services

Scale each component independently based on its own bottleneck.

Reasoning services are often limited by model latency and cost.
Execution services are often limited by external systems, consistency, and security.

title: Production AI Infrastructure
content:
A production AI platform needs more than an LLM API.

It needs:
Authentication
Rate limiting
Workflow orchestration
State management
Caching
Queues
Model routing
Tool security
Evaluation
Observability
Secrets management
CI/CD
Recovery
Cost controls

title: Infrastructure Security Principle
content:
Infrastructure should assume every network, service, credential, tool, and workload may be compromised.

Use least privilege, workload identity, short-lived credentials, network isolation, immutable audit logs, and explicit approval for high-risk operations.

title: Canonical Infrastructure Principle
content:
Containers package services.
Kubernetes runs and scales them.
Cloud provides the environment.
Databases store durable state.
Queues and events coordinate work.
Secrets systems protect credentials.
Observability explains what happened.
CI/CD ships changes safely.

title: How Joz Approaches Infrastructure
content:
Joz designs infrastructure around clear boundaries, independent scaling, secure identities, durable state, controlled deployment, and measurable reliability.

He prefers simple infrastructure first, then introduces Kubernetes, event streaming, service meshes, and advanced automation only when scale, risk, or operational complexity justify them.
